response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I know everyone talks about fake news and all that nonsense on social media, but honestly, I dodged that whole mess by just not being on any of those platforms. No Facebook, no Instagram, nothing. People think I'm strange, especially since I'm so social in person, but I tell them straight up that I don't have time for that garbage. I've got my kid to raise, my work at the garage, and my football matches to watch. Why would I want to add more stress to my life worrying about what's real and what's fake online? From what I hear from my coworkers and other parents at my kid's school, it's a nightmare. Everyone's sharing stuff without checking if it's true, getting worked up over things that never happened, spreading panic about everything. It makes me anxious just hearing about it. The way I see it, if you're going to use social media, you need to be skeptical about everything. Don't just believe something because it has a nice picture or because your friend shared it. I learned this the hard way when I first moved to Milan and people would tell me things about how things work here that turned out to be completely wrong. You have to verify things yourself. Ask questions. Check multiple sources. But honestly, the best solution is what I do, just stay off those platforms entirely. I get my news from television and newspapers, I talk to real people face to face. When I'm at the bar watching Inter play, I have actual conversations with people I can see and challenge if they're talking rubbish. That's real communication. My kid sometimes shows me things from their friends' social media, and half of it looks fake to me right away. I tell them to be careful, to think critically, but I worry because young people are so trusting. The whole system seems designed to make people anxious and confused, and I already deal with enough anxiety in my daily life without adding that digital chaos. If people must use social media, they should limit their time, question everything, and remember that most of what they see is either exaggerated or completely made up to get attention.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 47-year-old mechanic living in Milan who has no social media presence, which is the most critical factor for this topic. They have high extraversion (83) suggesting they're socially active in person, high neuroticism (71) indicating anxiety proneness, and low agreeableness (12) suggesting they're direct and skeptical. With low openness (28), they prefer traditional methods and are resistant to new platforms. Their primary education level and immigrant status inform a practical, street-smart perspective. The anxiety trait combined with no social media use creates a viewpoint of someone who has consciously avoided these platforms partly due to stress concerns. Their high extraversion means they value real-world social interaction over digital. Low agreeableness translates to blunt, critical opinions about social media users. The tone should be direct, somewhat dismissive of social media, practical, and slightly worried about others (especially their child). They would emphasize personal experience, face-to-face verification, and traditional media consumption.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, skeptical, somewhat dismissive, practical, anxious undertones, conversational but blunt
